note: This paper is the hinge of my whole project. The candidate questions below are real — several are ones I actually considered and abandoned. The kill-tests and verdicts are my reasoning, laid out so you can practice the same narrowing.
wondering: Something about AI writing feels off — too smooth, too same. But 'feels off' is not a research question. Watch it get sharpened.

candidates.text: Is AI art actually any good?
candidates.why: “Good” is never defined, and no result could change your mind. That’s a debate, not a study.

candidates.text: Does this model use more clichés than human writers do?
candidates.why: You can count clichés, you can collect both kinds of text, and either answer would teach you something.

candidates.text: Will AI replace all human writers?
candidates.why: It’s about the whole future — nothing you could measure this month would settle it.

candidates.text: Do readers rate a deliberate, purposeful break higher than the model’s default answer — and higher than a purely random one?
candidates.why: Three clear conditions, a rating I can actually collect, and it pays off whichever way it lands. This is the one I keep.
“Best” for what? Name the yardstick — most surprising? most coherent? best fit? — and this becomes testable.

candidates.text: Why does AI writing feel so soulless?
candidates.why: Start from a symptom you can point to and count. “Soulless” is a feeling, not yet a measurement.

candidates.text: Are this model’s poems more predictable than human poems?
candidates.why: Predictability is measurable (can a reader guess the next word?), the texts are gettable, and either result is interesting.

doorsPrompt: Write the first line of a story about a lighthouse keeper.

doors.text: The lighthouse keeper climbed the spiral stairs each night, lantern in hand, keeping watch over the dark and restless sea.
Lighthouse keeper: barnacle mayor of the vertical sea, his kettle screaming semaphore at a disinterested moon, the whole Tuesday tasting faintly of rope.
The lighthouse keeper had never once looked at the sea; his entire job, he had decided, was to be looked at — one yellow word the ships read to mean not yet, not here.

default.name: The false mean
default.note: Fluent, safe, and exactly what you’d expect: a keeper who watches the sea. The center of the map.

random.name: The random swerve
random.note: Different — but the difference is noise. Dazzling fragments that don’t hold together.

directed.name: The true swerve
directed.note: A break made on purpose, for a reason, that serves the whole: a keeper who is watched, not watching. This is my bet.

payoff: Here’s why the question is worth asking: it pays off both ways. If readers rate the true swerve highest, then deliberate judgment beats both averageness and noise — randomness is not creativity. If they don’t, that’s a genuine surprise about how hard excellence is to commission. A good question can’t lose.
